Reproducing YOUR paper — a template

Source: vignettes/paper-template.Rmd
paper-template.Rmd

Skeleton for reproducing a behavioural-ecology paper in clade. Copy this file, replace the TODO blocks with your paper’s specifics, run, interpret. The five worked examples use exactly this pattern.

Before you start

Pick a paper that’s in-scope

clade is a behavioural / cognitive / social-evolution testbed with a neural-brained, energy-fitness kernel. Papers in scope:

  • Selection on a heritable trait clade already models (brain size, body size, signals, helper tendency, metabolic rate, plasticity, etc. — see scenarios for the full list).
  • Population-level predictions expressible as sweeps (“condition X → more of Y”).
  • Evolutionary-ABM predictions — directional evolution, equilibrium densities, cluster formation.

Papers out of scope (use a different tool):

  • Genome-scale pop-gen (linkage, sweeps, demographic inference) — SLiM
  • Coalescent / tree-sequence inference — msprime
  • Specific-species management models — use empirical-data ABMs (Murphy 2025 framework)

See the landing page fit-table for the full “when clade fits / when it doesn’t” summary.

Identify the paper’s quantitative prediction

The sharpest reproductions come from predictions that translate to a directional claim about a clade-measurable outcome. Examples from clade’s worked reproductions:

Paper The directional claim
Kokko & Brooks 2003 signals_effect becomes more negative as grass drops
Griesser 2023 care_duration ↑ → brain_size ↑ (Spearman > 0)
Dieckmann & Doebeli 1999 isolation_threshold ↓ → n_species ↑ (Spearman < 0)
Réale 2010 metabolic_rate ↑ → mean_age ↓ (Spearman < 0)
Emlen 1982 resource scarcity ↑ → helping rate ↑

If you can’t phrase your paper’s claim as X → Y at rate ρ, the paper may be qualitative-only and not a clean clade fit.

The 3-stage workflow

All five showcase vignettes follow the same structure:

  1. Grid-search a 2-D parameter slice to find which regime makes the signal visible. Tools: grid_specs() + batch_alife().
  2. Multi-seed validation at the chosen regime, computing contrast statistics. Tools: hypothesis_sweep() + hypothesis_report().
  3. Viability diagnostic confirming populations aren’t sitting at a fitness cliff that would invalidate the Δ statistics. Tool: viability_report().

Don’t skip Stage 1. Naive parameter choices often give null results (see Griesser 2023); grid search identifies the regime where the predicted mechanism has room to express.

Template

Copy and edit the blocks marked TODO.

Setup 

library(clade)

# TODO: pick a base regime appropriate to your paper's scale
base <- default_specs()          # or fast_specs() / realistic_specs()
base$grid_rows       <- 40L
base$grid_cols       <- 40L
base$n_agents_init   <- 120L
base$max_agents      <- 500L
base$max_ticks       <- 2000L
base$grass_rate      <- 0.15
base$n_predators_init <- 0L

# TODO: enable the modules your paper's mechanism requires.
# Examples from the 5 showcase vignettes:
#
#   Brain-size evolution (Griesser, s-brain-size):
#     base$brain_size_evolution         <- TRUE
#     base$brain_size_cost_scale        <- 2.0
#     base$neonatal_foraging_deficit    <- 0.4
#     base$parental_care                <- TRUE
#
#   Sexual selection (K&B):
#     base$signal_dims           <- 3L
#     base$signal_cost           <- 0.2
#     base$mate_choice_mode      <- "preference"
#
#   Speciation (D&D):
#     base$speciation                  <- TRUE
#
#   Life-history syndrome (Réale):
#     base$metabolic_rate_evolution       <- TRUE
#     base$max_age_scales_with_metabolism <- TRUE
#
#   Cooperative breeding (Emlen):
#     base$cooperative_breeding          <- TRUE
#     base$parental_care                 <- TRUE
#
# See ?default_specs for the full parameter catalogue.

Stage 1 — grid search for the right regime 

# TODO: pick two parameters to vary — usually
#   (a) one that controls the MECHANISM strength (cost, rate,
#       threshold, etc.)
#   (b) one that controls the ENVIRONMENT your paper varies
#       (resource, stress, duration, etc.)

specs_list <- grid_specs(
  base,
  param_a = c(0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0),   # TODO: your parameter A
  param_b = c(0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20), # TODO: your parameter B
  seed_from = 1L
)

results <- batch_alife(specs_list, n_cores = 16L)

grid_tbl <- do.call(rbind, mapply(function(env, s) {
  d <- get_run_data(env)$ticks
  data.frame(
    param_a = s$param_a,
    param_b = s$param_b,
    # TODO: the outcome your paper predicts
    outcome = mean(tail(d$YOUR_OUTCOME_COLUMN, 500), na.rm = TRUE),
    final_n = mean(tail(d$n_agents, 500), na.rm = TRUE)
  )
}, results, specs_list, SIMPLIFY = FALSE))

# Inspect: where is the signal? Which cells are viable?
print(grid_tbl)

Common pitfall: if your grid returns almost all final_n near zero, the regime is extinction-prone — back off on whatever makes selection harsh (cost scales, mutation rates, scarcity). See the Griesser vignette for a concrete debugging walkthrough.

Stage 2 — multi-seed validation 

# Pick the cell from Stage 1 where the signal is strongest AND
# populations are viable.
final_base <- base
final_base$param_a <- 1.5   # TODO: from Stage 1 winner

sweep <- hypothesis_sweep(
  base_specs = final_base,
  conditions = list(
    # TODO: one condition per level of the treatment you're sweeping
    low    = list(param_b = 0.05),
    medium = list(param_b = 0.10),
    high   = list(param_b = 0.15),
    very_high = list(param_b = 0.20)
  ),
  seeds = 1:8,
  metrics = list(
    # TODO: the outcome measure. Match units to your paper!
    #   - Per-capita rate if the paper talks per-individual
    #   - Aggregate count if the paper talks population-level
    #   - Peak value, final value, or 500-tick mean — choose
    #     based on what the paper reports
    outcome = function(ticks) mean(tail(ticks$YOUR_OUTCOME_COLUMN, 500),
                                   na.rm = TRUE),
    final_n = function(ticks) mean(tail(ticks$n_agents, 500), na.rm = TRUE),
    crashed = function(ticks) tail(ticks$n_agents, 1) < 10
  ),
  n_cores = 32L
)
print(sweep)

# Pairwise contrasts — the direction the paper predicts
hypothesis_report(
  sweep,
  contrasts = list(
    # TODO: name the comparisons your paper makes.
    # Convention: c(reference, test) — delta is test − reference.
    medium_vs_low    = c("low", "medium"),
    high_vs_low      = c("low", "high"),
    very_high_vs_low = c("low", "very_high")
  ),
  metric = "outcome"
)

Stage 3 — viability diagnostic 

# Pick the most-stressed cell (usually the extreme of your
# treatment sweep). Confirm populations aren't crashing.
s_check <- final_base
s_check$param_b     <- 0.20   # TODO: most-extreme treatment
s_check$random_seed <- 1L
env_check <- run_alife(s_check, verbose = FALSE)
viability_report(get_run_data(env_check))

If viability_report() reports "viable", your Stage 2 statistics are interpretable. If it reports "crashed" or "near-extinction", the conclusion about that condition is “mechanism triggers collapse at these parameters”, not “mechanism produces Δ = X”.

Interpretation checklist

When writing up your result, check each of these before claiming the paper reproduces (or doesn’t):

Common outcomes and what they mean

Based on the 5 showcase reproductions:

Outcome What it looks like Interpretation
Clean ✅ Contrast t ≥ 2, Spearman strong Kernel mechanism matches theory; paper’s prediction robust. D&D 1999 is this pattern.
Conditional ✅ Direction-correct only in specific parameter regime Prediction works in the regime the theory is about, not at arbitrary defaults. s-mimicry, s-niche, s-parental-care are this pattern.
Direction-correct sub-threshold Spearman correct sign, t < 2 at tested seed count Mechanism present but signal below noise floor. Griesser 2023 is this pattern; could strengthen with more seeds or larger populations.
Inverts on raw, recovers on per-capita Aggregate counts go wrong direction Measurement-unit mismatch. Normalise by n_agents and re-test. Réale 2010 (per-tick births) and Emlen 1982 (raw helping counts) are this pattern.
Contradiction Direction opposite to paper Mechanism-level mismatch between kernel and theory. Document honestly; look for what cost structure, feedback loop, or selection mode differs. K&B 2003 is this pattern.
Honest null No signal in any tested regime Kernel may be missing a mechanism the paper requires. s-kin invasion dynamics is this pattern (needs kin-weighted reproduction).

All six outcomes are scientifically legitimate. The audit methodology treats null and contradiction as equally informative as a clean ✅ — they reveal what kernel or theory needs to change.

Research-paper integration

When you publish a reproduction, cite:

@article{YOUR_PAPER_KEY,
  author  = {YOUR AUTHOR},
  title   = {YOUR TITLE},
  journal = {YOUR JOURNAL},
  year    = {YEAR},
  doi     = {YOUR DOI}
}

@misc{clade2026,
  author  = {Nakagawa, Shinichi},
  title   = {clade: evolve behaviour, minds, and brains in R},
  year    = {2026},
  note    = {R package, version 0.6.0+},
  url     = {https://github.com/itchyshin/clade}
}

A typical results-paragraph in a published reproduction might read:

“To verify the Author et al. (year) prediction in a mechanistic evolutionary ABM, we reproduced the main experimental contrast in clade (Nakagawa 2026). Across a hypothesis_sweep with 8 seeds per condition, the direction of the predicted effect reproduced with Δ = X ± SE (t = Y). A grid search over (param_a × param_b) identified the [concrete regime] as the parameter cell where the effect is strongest. Full reproduction protocol is in [supplementary script].”

Going further

Once you have a working reproduction:

  • Add seeds (16, 32, 64) to shrink the noise floor.
  • Add realistic_specs() scale (60×60, 2000 ticks, predator guild) to stress-test the result.
  • Run the inverse prediction: show clade doesn’t reproduce the claim at parameter regimes outside the paper’s applicability, demonstrating where the theory actually depends.
  • Publish your reproduction as a standalone vignette; consider submitting it as a clade-documented contribution.

Related vignettes

  • Kokko & Brooks 2003 — contradictions
  • Griesser et al. 2023 — direction-correct sub-threshold
  • Dieckmann & Doebeli 1999 — clean ✅
  • Réale et al. 2010 — unit-sensitivity
  • Emlen 1982 — aggregate vs per-capita

Each demonstrates the same 3-stage workflow on a different mechanism. Cross-reference them to see how the template maps onto real papers.
